sections.heading: Why and How God Should Be Loved
sections.html:
The Reason and Measure of Love

Bernard opens with a question that is also its own answer: Why should God be loved? Because he is God. The cause of loving God is God himself; the measure of loving God is to love him without measure. This deceptively simple formulation resists two opposite errors. Against cold rationalism, it insists that love of God is not proportional to a calculated ledger of benefits received but is a response adequate to the infinite dignity of the one loved. Against quietist passivity, it insists that love is not a static attitude but a dynamic movement of the whole person toward its supreme good.

Bernard grounds this in Deuteronomy 6:5, the Shema's command to love the Lord with all the heart, soul, and strength — a command that by its very totality excludes any competing claim. 1 John 4:19 adds the crucial asymmetry: "We love because he first loved us." Love of God is always responsive before it is initiative; it is awakened by a prior gift, not self-generated by moral effort.

God Meets Man Where He Is: The Incarnation

Bernard is a thoroughly Christocentric writer, and nowhere more so than in his account of how love begins. He acknowledges that human beings are, by nature and by sin, curved inward — loving themselves first and tending to extend love outward only insofar as things serve self-interest. Rather than condemning this as simply wicked, Bernard reads it as the raw material of spiritual growth. God condescends to meet humanity in its self-love by becoming a man whom human beings can love humanly.

The Incarnation is thus pedagogically strategic. John 1:14 — "The Word became flesh and dwelt among us" — means that the invisible, intangible God has made himself visible, tangible, and loveable in human terms. The infant in the manger, the teacher walking dusty roads, the crucified Savior — these scenes kindle affective love in souls not yet capable of purely spiritual contemplation. Bernard's exegesis of the Song of Songs, which occupies his later years, expresses the same conviction: the soul must first love the historical, human Christ before it can be raised to love the eternal Word.

This carnal beginning of love is not condemned but expected. 1 Corinthians 15:46 — "It is not the spiritual that is first but the natural" — provides Bernard with a theological license for the soul's gradual ascent. The four degrees of love he outlines are not four separate paths but one ascending arc, and the Incarnation is what makes the arc possible.

The Four Degrees Introduced

Bernard introduces the four degrees as a framework for describing the soul's journey toward God. They move from self-love as the soul's inescapable starting point, through instrumental love of God for benefits received, to pure love of God for his own sake, and finally to the eschatological summit: love of self solely for God's sake. Each degree does not so much displace the previous one as transfigure it. The self is not ultimately destroyed but finally rightly ordered — loved in God and for God rather than loved as an end in itself.

The scriptural axis of the four degrees is the double commandment of Matthew 22:37–39: love of God with the whole being and love of neighbor as oneself. Bernard reads these not as two simultaneous static obligations but as describing a trajectory in which love of self, rightly ordered by love of God, eventually becomes love of neighbor and finally love of self sub specie Dei — under the aspect of God. The goal is not selflessness in the sense of self-obliteration, but selflessness in the sense of perfect reorientation.

  • Deut. 6:5
  • 1 John 4:19
  • John 1:14
  • 1 Cor. 15:46
  • Matt. 22:37–39

sections.heading: The Four Degrees of Love
sections.html:
First and Second Degrees: Self-Love and Beneficiary Love

The first degree of love is loving oneself for one's own sake. Bernard does not treat this as a perversity to be immediately overcome but as the necessary and unavoidable beginning of the creature's existence. The self is given; self-preservation is natural. The problem arises when this self-love expands beyond its proper sphere and begins to consume what belongs to neighbors and to God. Bernard cites Romans 13:9 — "You shall love your neighbor as yourself" — noting that the self-love implicit in "as yourself" is assumed and not condemned; it is the unchecked excess of self-love that the commandment corrects.

Experience teaches the person locked in the first degree that self-sufficiency is an illusion. Hardship, sickness, and the limits of human power drive the soul to seek help beyond itself. Here God's providential generosity intervenes: he provides food, healing, consolation, community. The soul, receiving these gifts, begins to love the giver — not yet for who the giver is in himself, but for what the giver supplies. This is the second degree: loving God for God's benefits. Bernard does not disparage this degree; it is honest, it is the beginning of a real relationship, and it is precisely where the majority of believers live most of the time. Psalm 34:8 — "Taste and see that the Lord is good" — describes the soul at this second degree: it has tasted goodness and found it satisfying, though it has not yet been captured by the goodness itself.

Third Degree: Loving God for God's Own Sake

The transition from the second to the third degree is not a voluntary achievement but a gift of grace. As the soul continues to draw near to God — through Scripture, through sacrament, through contemplative prayer, through suffering willingly borne — it begins to perceive God not merely as a benefactor but as Beauty itself, Truth itself, the supreme Good in whom all lesser goods participate. The soul's love is no longer primarily motivated by what it receives but by what God is.

Bernard draws on the language of the Song of Songs, where the beloved is drawn not by gifts but by the Bridegroom himself: "Draw me after you; let us run" — a running not away from the Bridegroom but into his embrace. The soul at the third degree has discovered that God is not merely useful but loveable, not merely powerful but beautiful. Psalm 73:25 expresses this degree with precision: "Whom have I in heaven but you? And there is nothing on earth that I desire besides you." The psalmist has moved past needing God's gifts to needing God himself.

This is the degree that, Bernard insists, is fully attainable in the present life and is the normal goal of Christian sanctification. The fourth degree belongs primarily to eternity, though rare souls are granted brief foretastes of it in intense moments of contemplation — what Bernard elsewhere calls the raptus, a momentary experience of union with God in which the soul loses itself in him as a drop of water is lost in wine.

Fourth Degree: Loving Self Only for God's Sake

The fourth and highest degree — loving oneself solely for God's sake — is the eschatological consummation of the soul's journey. In this state, the soul does not cease to exist or to care for itself, but it refers everything about itself, including its own existence and beatitude, entirely to God. Self-love is not eliminated but perfectly subordinated and redirected. The soul that has arrived at this degree cannot will anything except what God wills; it loves the self that God loves and for the reason God loves it — as the creature called into being to glorify and enjoy its Creator.

Bernard acknowledges the rarity of this attainment in the present life. Philippians 1:23 — Paul's desire "to depart and be with Christ, for that is far better" — represents a soul approaching this fourth degree: the apostle's love for himself has been so thoroughly reconfigured by love of Christ that even the desire for continued embodied existence must yield to the pull of union with Christ. Bernard also cites Romans 9:3 in paradoxical counterpoint: Paul's willingness to be "accursed from Christ" for the sake of his brothers — a statement that, for Bernard, expresses not a lesser love but the fourth degree's characteristic self-abnegation, where even one's own salvation is held as a trust from God rather than a private possession.

  • Ps. 34:8
  • Ps. 73:25
  • Song 1:4
  • Phil. 1:23
  • Rom. 9:3

sections.heading: Love, Humility, and the Blessed Life
sections.html:
The Body's Resurrection and the Soul's Final Rest

Bernard addresses a question his analysis of the four degrees raises: if the soul in its disembodied state after death is fully united to God, is not the resurrection of the body a step backward — a reattachment to the material that would distract from pure contemplation? His answer is a resounding no, drawing on the Pauline conviction of 1 Corinthians 15:42–44 that the resurrection body is not the same as the present body but a spiritual body, glorified, incorruptible, fitted for the life of the age to come.

Bernard argues that the soul, though it can be united to God in a certain way apart from the body, is not fully itself without it. The resurrection completes the person. Moreover, the glorified body will not compete with the soul's love of God but will become a pure vehicle for it. The saints' love in the final resurrection state will therefore be fuller than the disembodied love of souls awaiting resurrection, for they will love God with the whole person — body and soul — in perfect and everlasting harmony. Romans 8:23 — "we wait eagerly for adoption as sons, the redemption of our bodies" — expresses this longing for the complete eschatological gift.

How Love of God Transforms All Desires

Bernard draws a sweeping portrait of the transformed soul. When love of God reaches its fullness, no desire remains that is not caught up into that love. The hunger for beauty, for knowledge, for friendship, for rest — all these natural human desires are not destroyed but fulfilled, purified, and elevated. Bernard reads Psalm 16:11 — "In your presence there is fullness of joy; at your right hand are pleasures forevermore" — as the scripture of the beatific life: not the extinction of desire but its perfect and permanent satisfaction in God.

This transformation requires humility as its indispensable precondition. Bernard's companion treatise On the Steps of Humility and Pride is the necessary counterpart to On Loving God: the soul cannot ascend toward God in love while simultaneously descending into pride and self-assertion. James 4:6 — "God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble" — describes a structural feature of the divine economy: the proud soul, curved inward, cannot receive the love that is being freely offered from without. Humility is not self-deprecation but truthfulness about one's creatureliness, and this truthfulness opens the soul to receive what it cannot generate from itself.

The Martyrs and a Concluding Hymn to Charity

Bernard appeals to the witness of the martyrs as the most visible historical demonstration of love at or near the fourth degree. Those who went to death rather than deny Christ did not merely calculate that eternal reward outweighed temporal suffering; they had been so consumed by love of Christ that the self's natural interest in self-preservation had yielded entirely to the Beloved. Romans 8:38–39 — Paul's hymn that neither death nor life nor anything in creation can separate the believer from the love of God in Christ Jesus — reads, in Bernard's interpretation, as a description of the martyr's interior state: not defiance of death, but love that has made death irrelevant.

The treatise closes with what is effectively a meditation on charity as the name of God's own inner life poured into human souls. 1 John 4:8 — "God is love" — is for Bernard not merely a doctrinal statement but an invitation: if God is love, then to grow in love is to grow in God, and to reach the fullness of love is to be, in the deepest sense, in God and God in us. The whole arc of the four degrees is nothing other than the creature's journey into the truth of this one sentence — not arriving at a static destination but entering more and more fully into the inexhaustible divine reality that is charity itself.

  • 1 Cor. 15:42–44
  • Rom. 8:23
  • Ps. 16:11
  • James 4:6
  • Rom. 8:38–39
  • 1 John 4:8
